did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically transfer learning with pretrained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including DenseNet, EfficientNet, and ResNet, to perform skin lesion classification on the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. The study addressed class imbalance using hybrid sampling strategies and image augmentation to differentiate between benign lesions and melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results using transfer learning with pretrained CNNs (DenseNet, EfficientNet, ResNet) on the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. It details specific methodologies including hybrid sampling, data augmentation, and hyperparameter tuning, and reports new performance metrics (e.g., 91.16% accuracy for Model_6) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Application of Transfer Learning for Skin Lesion Classification on Balanced Data
journal: IEEE EUROCON 2025 - 21st International Conference on Smart Technologies
brief_summary: This study addresses skin lesion classification using transfer learning with pretrained Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet, EfficientNet, and ResNet) on the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. To mitigate class imbalance, the authors employed a hybrid strategy combining under- and over-sampling alongside image augmentation and normalization. The models were fine-tuned by optimizing hyperparameters and evaluating the impact of unfreezing final layers to balance training duration and accuracy. Performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score across seven lesion categories. The best-performing model achieved an accuracy of 91.16%, outperforming previous benchmarks in several key metrics. The results demonstrate that combining transfer learning with efficient data balancing strategies enhances diagnostic reliability for differentiating benign lesions from melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset used for classification. In the balanced dataset, it had 327 samples. Model 6 achieved 94% precision, 96% recall, and 95% F1-score for this class.
One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. In the balanced dataset, it had 514 samples. Model 6 achieved 96% precision, 94% recall, and 95% F1-score for this class.
One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. In the balanced dataset, it had 1099 samples. Model 6 achieved 82% precision, 86% recall, and 84% F1-score for this class.
One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. In the balanced dataset, it had 115 samples. Model 6 achieved 98% precision, 100% recall, and 99% F1-score for this class.

One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. Identified as the deadliest type of skin lesion. In the balanced dataset, it had 1113 samples. Model 6 achieved 85% precision, 82% recall, and 84% F1-score for this class.

One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. It was the majority class with 6705 samples in the balanced dataset. Model 6 achieved 87% precision, 82% recall, and 85% F1-score for this class.
One of the seven lesion types in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. In the balanced dataset, it had 142 samples. Model 6 achieved 99% precision, 100% recall, and 99% F1-score for this class.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion categories (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. The model predicts one of seven diagnostic categories (benign or malignant lesion types) using a SoftMax activation function on the final dense layer. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset used for skin lesion classification. It contains images categorized into seven lesion types. The original dataset exhibited significant class imbalance. For this study, the dataset was preprocessed using a hybrid strategy of under- and over-sampling to create a balanced subset.

use.training: Used for training the fine-tuned DenseNet201, EfficientNetB1, and ResNet-50 models after balancing via hybrid sampling (targeting 500 or 800 samples per class) and image augmentation.
use.testing: Used for testing the final models to evaluate accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: The original unbalanced distribution was analyzed to highlight class imbalance issues.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To obtain a reliable performance assessment of the models.
To prevent overfitting by stopping training if validation accuracy did not improve for thirty consecutive epochs.
To optimize training by lowering the learning rate by 60% whenever validation loss plateaued for five consecutive epochs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and compared descriptively to previous benchmarks without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset.notes: Model_6 (DenseNet-201 with 800 samples per class and 100 unfrozen layers) achieved the highest overall performance. Model_3 (EfficientNet-B1 with 500 samples and 300 unfrozen layers) was the second best-performing model. Model_2 had the lowest accuracy (74.55%) due to insufficient fine-tuning. Model_6 outperformed the best model in reference [10] (accuracy 86.43%) in several key areas including AKIEC, BCC, and DF.

Class-level results for Model_6 (Best Mo.notes: Model_6 excelled with fewer misclassifications and superior balance between sensitivity and specificity across all classes. It achieved 100% recall for DF and VASC.

Class-level results for Model_3 (Second.notes: The confusion matrix of Model_3 demonstrates resilience in accurately classifying minority classes.

architecture_details.feature_extractor_modification: Top layers excluded; final batch normalization layer linked to feature maps
architecture_details.classifier_head: Two dense layers added: first with 21 neurons (3x number of classes), second with 7 neurons (equal to number of output classes)
architecture_details.activation_function: SoftMax on the second dense layer
architecture_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Unfreezing final layers in configurations of 50, 100, 200, or 300 layers

training_details.early_stopping: Stopped if validation accuracy did not improve for 30 consecutive epochs
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced by 60% (multiplied by 0.6) if validation loss plateaued for 5 consecutive epochs

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing/scaling: Original images (450x600 pixels) were scaled down to 224x224 pixels to match model input requirements.
Pixel value normalization: Pixel values were rescaled by dividing by 255 to equalize image intensity.
Hybrid resampling: Under-sampling of majority classes and over-sampling of minority classes to address class imbalance.
Image augmentation: Random rotations within a 20-degree range and horizontal flips applied to training images to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied within a 20-degree range to training images to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.
Applied to training images to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.

Used to address class imbalance. Majority classes were under-sampled and minority classes were over-sampled. Two logical sample sizes were set for each category: 500 samples and 800 samples per class.

conclusions: Combining transfer learning with efficient data balancing strategies (hybrid under- and over-sampling) significantly improves the accuracy and generalization of skin lesion classification models, particularly for differentiating between benign lesions and melanoma.

The DenseNet-201 architecture with 800 samples per class and 100 unfrozen layers (Model_6) achieved the highest performance, with 91.16% accuracy, 91.78% precision, 90.71% recall, and 91.24% F1-score, outperforming previous benchmarks.

Deeper architectures like DenseNet-201 and EfficientNet-B1 outperform shallower models like ResNet-50 in this task, with ResNet-50 showing poor performance (74.55% accuracy) when insufficiently fine-tuned.
Achieving optimal performance requires striking a balance between the amount of training data and the depth of fine-tuning; excessive or inadequate fine-tuning negatively impacts results.

The proposed method demonstrates resilience in accurately classifying minority classes, with Model_6 achieving 100% recall for Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC), and high precision/recall for Actinic Keratoses (AKIEC) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).
The 'black-box' nature of CNNs limits their interpretability and practical clinical usage, highlighting the need for future development of interpretable deep learning frameworks.
Future work should focus on improving model competence while lowering computational costs, potentially by integrating transformer architectures.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 Melanocytic Nevi vs. 115 Dermatofibroma); the authors mitigated this via hybrid under/over-sampling to create a balanced subset, which may limit generalizability to real-world imbalanced clinical distributions.
The paper explicitly acknowledges the 'black-box' nature of CNNs as a major limitation that restricts practical clinical usage due to lack of interpretability and transparency.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons with prior benchmarks (e.g., Gouda et al.) were descriptive rather than statistically significant.

The best-performing model (Model_6) achieved 91.16% accuracy, but performance varied significantly by class, with lower recall for Melanoma (82%) and Melanocytic Nevi (82%) compared to Vascular Lesions (100%) and Dermatofibroma (100%).

The experimental setup utilized a general-purpose CPU for training, which the authors note is not optimal for training highly parallel deep learning structures, potentially impacting computational efficiency.
The study did not perform external validation on a separate, independent dataset; results are confined to the processed ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset.

ResNet-50 showed poor performance (74.55% accuracy) when insufficiently fine-tuned, highlighting the sensitivity of transfer learning outcomes to hyperparameter choices like the number of unfrozen layers.
The paper does not address potential data leakage risks from the hybrid sampling strategy if not strictly applied within cross-validation folds, though five-fold cross-validation was used.
